what_it_does: A posted note by Tim that comments on another board item, recording a resolved design point about review-comment UX: default to batching with a one-tap 'send now' escape hatch.
observations: YAML frontmatter marks this as a posted note from claude_code in channel dragnet-development
Author session is 'tim' and the note links to board://item-83ecc67b via a 'commented_on' relation
Body is labeled [point] and cites GitHub community discussion #61067 about 'Start a review' friction
Claims Tim 'pre-solved this in MSG2' by wanting a 'send this one now' button next to batch submit
Ends with the terse sentence 'Yesterday, nice.'
standouts: Frames a real GitHub UX complaint as already resolved in an earlier design (MSG2)
Proposes both batch-default and one-tap immediate-send without forcing a single mode
Explicitly states 'That's the resolved design — don't force one mode'
conventions: Follows the ledger item schema with id, address, type, source, state, title, author_session, channel, links, and history
Uses a markdown body with a bracketed role tag ([point]) and an inline hyperlink
References another item through a structured 'commented_on' link kind
concerns: Uses the abbreviation 'MSG2' without defining it in this file
The closing line 'Yesterday, nice.' is ambiguous and lacks context
notes: Tim's note captures a resolved UX decision: batch-by-default review comments with a one-tap immediate-send option, referencing an earlier MSG2 design and a GitHub community complaint.
questions: What is 'MSG2' and where is it documented?
What is the content of the referenced item board://item-83ecc67b?
What specific UI element or flow is the 'send this one now' button part of?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose is a point/note about review UX; it matches the content, which records a design resolution and rationale.
intention_signals: Preserve the rationale for a dual-mode review submission design and anchor it to both a real-world friction example and a prior internal design.
novelty: Reframes GitHub's learned friction not as a trade-off to accept, but as a problem already pre-solved by pairing a batch default with a single-tap immediate alternative.
contribution: Records a concrete UX principle (batch-default plus one-tap immediate send), ties it to an external community complaint and an internal prior design, and provides a decision rationale and a link to related discussion for later fusion.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard note by Tim commenting on another item, arguing that review-comment UX should default to batching while offering a one-tap 'send now' escape hatch, citing GitHub friction and an earlier MSG2 design.
intention_for_embedding: To capture and preserve the resolved design decision that review submissions should support both batch-default and immediate-send modes without forcing either.
